title: Everyday Slang
description: The casual Italian young locals actually say - greetings, reactions, and filler you will not find in a textbook.
situation: Hanging out with friends: reacting to news, making plans, and sounding natural instead of stiff.

phrases.tip: The default casual greeting and goodbye with friends. Use 'Salve' or 'Buongiorno' with strangers or in shops.
Literally 'how does it go?'. The all-purpose check-in. Reply is usually 'Tutto bene' (all good).
The go-to word for anything cool. 'Che figo!' = how cool! Northern roots but used everywhere now.

phrases.english: How awesome / What a great thing
phrases.tip: An enthusiastic reaction to good news or something impressive. 'Che figata!' = that's so cool!
A shrug turned into a sound. The perfect one-word reply when you have no clue. Italians use it constantly.
Hugely versatile: encouragement, disbelief, or gentle nagging. 'Ma dai!' = no way! / you're kidding!
For any chaotic or confusing situation. Literally a noisy mess. Mildly cheeky but everyday, not rude.

phrases.english: Sharp / On the ball / Take care
phrases.tip: Literally 'in leg'. Calls someone capable and switched-on. Also a warm sign-off: 'Stai in gamba!'
Youth slang for losing your temper or panicking. 'Sto sclerando' = I'm losing my mind. Very casual.
As a wistful exclamation it means 'if only!'. In a sentence it softens to 'maybe / perhaps'. Used everywhere.
Literally 'I'm dead'. The everyday way to say you're exhausted. 'Sono morto' is the fuller form.
Literally 'I'm at the green'. The standard casual way to say you have no money.
Dropped into speech as filler the way English uses 'like' or 'I mean'. Note the grave accent: 'cioè'.
Reassures someone to relax. 'Tranqui' is the clipped youth version. 'Stai tranquillo' = take it easy.
Casual way to arrange a hangout. 'Ci becchiamo dopo' = let's meet up later. Common in texts among friends.
Literally 'to smash'. High praise: 'Spacchi!' = you're killing it! For someone or something that's great.
